utterances: Thanks so much for being with us today, Brian.

Brian Lamb is the chairman and CEO of C-SPAN.
This is TALK OF THE NATION from NPR News.
I'm Lynn Neary.
Neal Conan will be back on Monday.
This is TALK OF THE NATION.
I'm Neal Conan in Washington.
For the first time in five years, the Supreme Court has agreed to hear an abortion case, specifically a case that raises the issues of parental notification and the health of the mother.
A New Hampshire law obliges doctors to inform the parents of a pregnant minor 48 hours before an abortion, unless there's a medical emergency.
Most states now have some kind of parental involvement law; some require notification of one or both parents, some require not just notification, but a parent's permission.
Most people wouldn't argue with a parent's right to know about their child's welfare, but not all teens live in supportive home environments.
Some argue that notification laws may drive girls in dysfunctional or abusive family situations to another state or even to unsafe back alley abortions.
First, we'll look into what's at stake legally, but for most of this hour, we'll focus on what happens before the law is even involved.
If you're a teen-ager or a parent who's faced this situation, we want to hear your story.
Did you tell your parents?
Did your daughter talk to you?
What did you talk about and what did you do?
Our number here in Washington is (800) 989-8255.
That's (800) 989-TALK.
Our e-mail address is totn@npr.org.
Later in the program, a critical referendum on the European Constitution this weekend.
Public opinion polls suggest that France will vote no.
David Savage joins us now.
He's the Supreme Court reporter for the Los Angeles Times.
And he's with us from his office here in Washington, DC.
Always nice to have you on the program, David.
Hi, Neal.
First of all, give us the specifics on this New Hampshire law and the challenge to it.
Right.
...in my particular case.
Good morning.

So, to be able to record ""Gal from Joe's,"" which I'd never sung alone, to be able to record ""I Wish I Would Know How It Feels To Be Free,"" or even, ""How Long Must I Wander?"" which has always been a favorite of mine since her ""Here Comes the Sun"" album came out which that song is from.
Don't You Pay Him No Mind"" has been a song I've loved since I first heard it and always wanted to do.
So it was very personal and it just so happens that the songs that I chose seem to have put together a wonderful CD that people seem to be enjoying very much.
Now, your CD opens with your mother introducing you at a concert in Ireland.
(Soundbite of Nina Simone Concert)
I would like to introduce you to my daughter.
Her name is Simone.
We have never sung on the stage together.
And now I'm going to play for her to sing a song to you.
What's the name of it, honey?
I forgot.
Music for Lovers.
Music for Lovers.
When was this, and what was the occasion?
It was July 24th, 1999.
I forget the day, and I forget the hour, but it was one of the most thrilling moments of my life.
I always wanted to share the stage with her.
I told my mom that I was her background singer.
I had always been her background singer, she just didn't know it.
So three days after my daughter was born in 1999, she flew to Chicago, and while she was there in Chicago with me, we went into the studio and sat down at the piano together.
And I got a chance to have my own Nina Simone request line, and she was amazed that I knew as many of her songs as I did.
And the favorite one of hers that I sang for her was ""Music For Lovers."" I sang and she played.
(Soundbite of song ""Music For Lovers"")
(Singing) And there's music for lovers in the hush-a-bye dreams of a child.
When the whole world discovers...
What's your favorite song?
You have rattled off a list of amazing songs, but if you had to pick one off of your album that you love to listen to yourself, what would it be?
All of them.
Feelin' Good.
Mmm hmm.
Why?
Towards the end of that song, it's very obvious that I began to tell my own story.
And when I get to the point where I say, ""Freedom is mine, and I know how I feel.
It's a new dawn.
It's a new day.
It's a new life for me,"" it exemplifies where I am in terms of my life right now.
And something just happened, and we caught it on tape.
And it's there on the CD, and when I sing that song now, which I close my show with, people seem to understand where I'm coming from.
It's not just a song that I'm covering of my mother's.
It's me letting people know where I'm at.
(Soundbite of song ""Feelin' Good"")
(Singing) Fish in the sea, you know how I feel.
River running free, you know how I feel.
Blossom on the tree, you know how I feel.
It's a new day.
It's a new dawn.
It's a new life for me, and I'm feelin' good.
Let's talk a little bit about your mother.
She's someone who sounds as if she was fierce from day one.
As a child, when she was giving a performance, she basically had a civil rights moment so black folks could sit in the front of a concert hall.
Was she as fierce in private as she was in public?
What do you think?
I assume so, but, you tell me.
It wasn't a mantle that she donned when she got on stage.
My mother was - she was real from the rooter to the tooter.
You must be thrilled that things have turned out as well as they have, given that you didn't go straight into this career.
Do you think that you would have always regretted it if you hadn't decided to pursue the music?
I think that there's a part of me that would have always wondered what might have been, most definitely.
I get such joy from singing, and I seem to be able to inspire joy in others when I sing that I just can't imagine doing anything different with my life, especially at this stage in my life.
I've taken a lot of detours to get here, and I've overcome many, many obstacles and many nay-sayers who said that I could never do it.
And it's just a wonderful, wonderful feeling to be self-actualizing at this point in my life and my career.
Tell me a little bit about the foundation, the Nina Simone Foundation.
Well, the Nina Simone Foundation was formed shortly after my mother died, in less than a week.

And the reason why I formed it at the time was so that people would have a place to send flowers and cards since mommy lived in France, and there was no real address here in America for them to send it to.
I had no idea what a foundation was.
I had no idea what a 501c3 was, much less that I would have to come up with a mission at the time.
So, I say all that to say that God works in mysterious ways.
And so right now, the mission of the foundation is to educate children through recreation and through music.
My mother's hope was to educate children primarily in Ghana and South Africa.
So I just broadened her wishes to encapsulate all African-American children as well as other children of ethnic backgrounds.
(Soundbite of song ""I Wish I Knew"")
(Singing) I wish I knew how it would feel to be free.
We were joined by Simone.
She's debuting her first solo album called ""Simone on Simone,"" a tribute to her mother, Nina Simone.
She joined us from our NPR Studios in New York.
(Singing) I wish I could say all the things that I should say.
Say them loud.
Say them clear for the whole round world to hear.
That's our show for today.
Thank you for sharing your time with us.
